title: Which philosophy does Evangelion resemble most
display_title: Which philosophy does Evangelion resemble most
wikitext:
[]: Which philosophy does Evangelion resemble most?             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Fri Aug 26, 2011 1:58 pm I'm going to use the philosophies of Kierkegaard, Nietzsche and Sartre as my points of comparison but feel free to bring any philosopher or school of philosophy into the fray. Imo Eva is most consistent with Sartre's thought, especially in the last two episodes. Granted, the idea that the laws of nature inhibit our freedom to some degree is somewhat un-Sartrean (although Sartre doesn't see the laws of nature as necessary or necessarily permanent), but Eva's message that only oneself can understand oneself is a central point of Sartre's philosophy. On the other hand, the solution to this problem in eps 25-26 is more Kierkegaardian: ruling out a real unity in interpersonal relationships and society in favor of just understanding one's own motives and commitments certainly undercuts Nietzsche and even isolation-absorbed Sartre, but is pretty much in tune with Kierkegaard's flimsy and unfortunately solipsistic resolve to stand alone in one's convictions (in Kierkegaard's case, unflinching commitment to God through subjective faith) and find contentment in self-understanding, with engagement in society and the existential conflicts it brings basically optional and unnecessary to one's self-development. So yeah: a Sartrean conflict and a lackadaisical attempt at solving it that resembles the less inspiring end of Kierkegaard's thought. (I'd like to explore the more positive parallels between Evangelion and Kierkegaard later.)

[]: Re: Which philosophy does Evangelion resemble most?             Quote Post by Allemann » Fri Aug 26, 2011 3:40 pm Born of Lilith wrote: Imo Eva is most consistent with Sartre's thought, especially in the last two episodes. Granted, the idea that the laws of nature inhibit our freedom to some degree is somewhat un-Sartrean (although Sartre doesn't see the laws of nature as necessary or necessarily permanent), but Eva's message that only oneself can understand oneself is a central point of Sartre's philosophy.

[]: Re: Which philosophy does Evangelion resemble most?             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Sat Aug 27, 2011 12:20 pm Allemann wrote: NGE is highly unsartrean, for in the case of humans essence does precede existence; and it also highly kierkegaardian, as humans were created by a very powerful, hidden, and rationally not completely comprehensible personal force.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Sat Aug 27, 2011 1:43 pm I never noticed it being implied that essence precedes existence in the Eva universe

[]:             Quote Post by Born of Lilith » Sat Aug 27, 2011 2:33 pm AuraTwilight wrote: Souls exist in a Chamber of Guf before being born.

[]: Re: Which philosophy does Evangelion resemble most?             Quote Post by Allemann » Sat Aug 27, 2011 3:57 pm Born of Lilith wrote: I never noticed it being implied that essence precedes existence in the Eva universe,

[]:             Quote Post by EvangelionOtaku » Mon Sep 05, 2011 3:43 am Keeping in mind that I make no distinction between philosophy and religion (to me, they are synonymous words to be used interchangeably, along with the word tradition), there are some things that would cause me to say Evangelion most closely resembles Gnosticism (or if you throw a fit about my wording, let's say Gnostic philosophy) but other things make me see it as more closely resembling an intense pantheism and animism such as Shintoism. We could say Gnostic-Shinto then. I also find it resembles Jungianism (which is not just a mode of psychology but Jung's writings represent a philosophy) and a lot of what Jung had to say, especially about the collective consciousness doctrine and individuation and nondualism (so like, an intense monism). So Gnostic-Jungian-Shinto.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Sep 05, 2011 4:26 am I'd actually have to say the show has the greatest philosophical debt to the Greek Stoics. The Stoics above all had a focus on the will and the need to conform with nature in a rather tao-like manner, to accept what is and do what is right because it is right, not because it is easy. They praised courage, wisdom, justice and self control, viewed ignorance of yourself or the world around you as the true cause of evil and suffering, and held suicide in most circumstances to be a betrayal of one's duties. Here, a few quotes from the wiki article on the subject: "If, therefore, any be unhappy, let him remember that he is unhappy by reason of himself alone." -Epictetus, paraphrasing EoTV "Outward things cannot touch the soul, not in the least degree; nor have they admission to the soul, nor can they turn or move the soul; but the soul turns and moves itself alone." -Marcus Aurelius, explaining AT fields to Kaworu "The point is not how long you live, but how nobly you live." -Seneca the Younger, Kaji's life coach.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Mon Sep 05, 2011 4:34 am NemZ wrote: I'd actually have to say the show has the greatest philosophical debt to the Greek Stoics.

[]:             Quote Post by EvangelionOtaku » Mon Sep 05, 2011 1:26 pm Allemann wrote: Only in the case they read the Stoics, something I doubt they did.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Tue Sep 06, 2011 2:30 pm EvangelionOtaku wrote: Or developed Stoic thought and philosophy on their own, independently without reading the ancient writings of the movement we call Stoicism, which is entirely possible. Thoughts spring up independently around the world, with or without exposure to ancient writings, all the time. It is absolutely possible that someone could, on their own thinking and intellect, develop what resembles Stoicism without having read any ancient writings. What Stoic writings did the first Stoic thinkers read? None, they developed it on their own, without the need for writings. It's called creativity and originality, even without knowing the history or if someone else thought of it before you.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Sep 06, 2011 4:30 pm Nothing in that is exclusive to the Greeks though. Like I said earlier, a big part of it sounds very much in tune with a Taoist view of interactions with nature. The rest is a mix of appreciation for reason as motivation rather than to avoid negative emotions and some generally universal social views on what it means to be a good person... nothing all that revolutionary.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Wed Sep 07, 2011 12:16 pm NemZ wrote: a big part of it sounds very much in tune with a Taoist view of interactions with nature.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Sep 07, 2011 2:43 pm Allemann wrote: Their similarities are superficial. You can't mistake a Stoic for a Taoist.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Thu Sep 08, 2011 12:58 pm Yes, but Evangelion is more about changing in the sphere of interpersonal relationships than discovering the underlying pattern of the world and from it derive the way one ought to live. I'm not sure if the notion of fate in the metaphysical sense is present in Eva, thought I do think inadvertently the Eva world is made as a deterministic world, which is contradictory with its message of self-determination (incidentally, also why it isn't a sartrean show).

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Sep 08, 2011 2:09 pm Allemann wrote: Yes, but Evangelion is more about changing in the sphere of interpersonal relationships than discovering the underlying pattern of the world and from it derive the way one ought to live.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Fri Sep 09, 2011 4:28 am NemZ wrote: If you include society as a whole and other people as factors of that outside world it very much does apply. What is holding the characters back is ignorance of their true selves and place in the system, the dichotomy between how others view them and how they view themselves.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri Sep 09, 2011 5:13 am Allemann wrote: There is no system in Evangelion where characters are placed, GNR notwithstanding.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Sat Sep 10, 2011 12:12 pm NemZ wrote: Any society is a system.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sat Sep 10, 2011 3:40 pm Allemann wrote: System is a very useful conceptual category to understand phenomena, but something having the property of a system doesn't preclude that it parts don't have a level of independence. A society of individuals who have the right to pursue their interests under the condition they don't infringe others in pursuing their interests still has a large space for personal independence and growth.

Imo Eva is most consistent with Sartre's thought, especially in the last two episodes. Granted, the idea that the laws of nature inhibit our freedom to some degree is somewhat un-Sartrean (although Sartre doesn't see the laws of nature as necessary or necessarily permanent), but Eva's message that only oneself can understand oneself is a central point of Sartre's philosophy. On the other hand, the solution to this problem in eps 25-26 is more Kierkegaardian: ruling out a real unity in interpersonal relationships and society in favor of just understanding one's own motives and commitments certainly undercuts Nietzsche and even isolation-absorbed Sartre, but is pretty much in tune with Kierkegaard's flimsy and unfortunately solipsistic resolve to stand alone in one's convictions (in Kierkegaard's case, unflinching commitment to God through subjective faith) and find contentment in self-understanding, with engagement in society and the existential conflicts it brings basically optional and unnecessary to one's self-development. So yeah: a Sartrean conflict and a lackadaisical attempt at solving it that resembles the less inspiring end of Kierkegaard's thought. (I'd like to explore the more positive parallels between Evangelion and Kierkegaard later.)
